author: @travismoore7849
text: I learned a long time ago that there is a trick to transforming DC power with a bifilar transformer. This is done by using a load matching the load you want to run on the output. this way you can mirror you loads. But it is not as efficient. But you can use a smaller coil in a large coil bifilar with a dummy load to have a high voltage on the output by wrapping it on a drum like the mystery crystal set radios. For pancakes you may try putting inductors in series on the output.

author: @murb2586
text:
again, thanks for your hard work and sharing with us, it means alot to me and im sure others.


it would be interesting to see if electric motor coils and magnets can be 3d printed (maybe hemp carbon) and painted, incorporating layering and rolling akin to windings to generate the fields, the printability can really lend itself to designing complex and maybe even dynamic (w/ arduinos) field line vectors and structures. maybe even adding capacitance to different areas of the motor to create a motor-battery to reduc resistance and complexity?  ok now im really winging it. 


but i'm personally imagining motors cocentrically within motors, each adding to and modifying the overall field shape as well increasing the rpm exponentially.  


just the cocentric design using even common ac and dc motor designs may be the key to electric "horsepower" or top-end power without complicated gearboxes, and packed into a hub motor may be a game changer, but im not an expert. 


anyway, thanks again and cheerios

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
For those interested, this coil has a reasonable amount of DC resistance to limit AC current. And if you want to know its inductance in henries, but dont have expensive equipment to measure it, then here's an alternative method to give you reasonably close. 

First, measure the coil's DC resistance. Then measure the AC current through it from a known AC voltage and known freqency. Caution must be observed to not supply a voltage that would push too much current through. One thought is to use a transformer that is used to convert 60hz 120ac  or 50 hz 240ac to 12vdc.  The transformer itself will probably output something like 18vac  you can further limit the current by adding a known non-wire wound resistor. 

Now knowing ac amps (Iac), and AC Voltage( Vac), you can calculate the AC impedence  Z=Vac/Iac.
Impedence, Z   also = sqrt((net of inductive and capacitive reactance)^2 + (total dc resistance)^2). For the most part, we can ignore capacitive reactance here at low frequencies and just work with  inductive reactance = XL = 2×Pi×Frequency×Inductance

Put together results in  Vac/Iac=Z=sqrt((2×Pi×F×L)^2 +(R)^2). Isolating for inductance  gives  L=Sqrt((Vac/Iac)^2‐(R)^2)/2×Pi×F.

replies.author: @jeffreyrood8755
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 well, it's one of those hobby ideas so time and money would be considered as that. I need one for my car and was thinking about it when I saw this video.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @jeffreyrood8755 Neat! As for inductive charging a car, your talking about non trivial amounts of power and you want to transfer it efficently.  The really big issue is significant currents and DC resistance in the coil can lead to significant amount of power loss and heating.  Higher freqency lower inductance coils save on copper requirements but start to experence skin effect  on the wires themselfs, where inductive reactance inside the wire tends to limit current flow to just the surface. Engineers compensate for skin effect by using litz wire, making a composite wire from many smaller insulated wires that take turns being on the surface.

author: @BeLowBelowo

replies.author: @boots7859
replies.text:
Well, it might if more technical details were given.  One day its batteries, the next super caps, the next hemp, the next graphene mag-air combo's, plating, etc.  Lately it seems like a big effort to start selling graphene ink from the store.
A lot of it sounds cool, however even after months and months of testing, still no data on simple longer term testing of a g-batt or g-cap.
Make one of each in the same size/form factor of a common 18650, or mod super-cap, run charge/discharge tests with an Arduino logged to a computer for a month or two or three and show some actual data.
When this is asked for, the reply is always "Buy some and try it yourself".....  This simply makes me suspect that some of these items are great to start with, but over a short amount of time simply become unstable, oxidized, or something else which limits their actual use.
The team has the ability to make a couple of devices and do the above testing laughably simply,  Since they're not stupid, and characterizing their products is simple, and necessesary if they want to talk to anyone in any industry, I can't help but think that they have done so, and realize at this stage its simply fit for hobbyest/tinkerers. After 6 months, 12 months of graphene super-caps, and some incredible claims, there are no realistic IP concernse, Patent theivery or other Proprietary rational for saying "We've got an equivelent to a Market Leader Maxwell 3V 3000F graphene super-cap that is 3000,10000, etc, which is remarkably cheaper or better in x/y/z parameter".
The Big Players in the industry are as able as you or I to order some product and test to get these answers, so again no rational reason for not publishing.  A lot of us have seen the zero-point energy scams, solar ones like Green and Gold, and all sorts of fantastic claims with no simple data sheets.  I hope I'm wrong, however does anyone really think Market Leaders haven't/aren't investigating Graphene for the past decade?
